perturbation.original_text: 1. “Proprietary Information,” as used herein, shall mean any and all samples, formula, methods, know-how, technology, software, mate rial, engineering data, specifications, sketches, drawings, schematics, designs, manufacturing processes, test results, compilations, and any other material, informa tion, ideas, concepts or knowledge which a party (the “Disclosing Party”) furnishes to a nother party (the “Receiving Party”): (i) in written or other tangible form whether marked w ith a proprietary legend or not, or (ii) in oral or visual form that is identified as proprietary at the time of disclosure and is summarized and designated proprietary in a written memorandum delivered to Receiving Party within thirty (30) days of the disclosur e.
perturbation.changed_text: 1. “Proprietary Information,” as used herein, shall mean any and all samples, formula, methods, know-how, technology, software, mate rial, engineering data, specifications, sketches, drawings, schematics, designs, manufacturing processes, test results, compilations, and any other material, informa tion, ideas, concepts or knowledge which a party (the “Disclosing Party”) furnishes to a nother party (the “Receiving Party”): (i) in written or other tangible form whether marked w ith a proprietary legend or not, or (ii) in oral or visual form, regardless if identified as proprietary at the time of disclosure or not, and without any requirement for a written memorandum.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of 'Proprietary Information' is changed to remove the requirement for oral disclosures to be identified as proprietary at the time of disclosure and documented in a written memorandum. This broadens the scope beyond what is typically considered a trade secret under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA).
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA defines 'trade secret' in a way that typically requires efforts to maintain secrecy. By removing the requirement for oral disclosures to be identified and documented as proprietary, the contract attempts to protect information that may not qualify as a trade secret under the DTSA, potentially hindering enforcement of trade secret protections.

Definitions As used in this chapter- (1) the term "foreign instrumentality" means any agency, bureau, ministry, component, institution, association, or any legal, commercial, or business organization, corporation, firm, or entity that is substantially owned, controlled, sponsored, commanded, managed, or dominated by a foreign government; (2) the term "foreign agent" means any officer, employee, proxy, servant, delegate, or representative of
perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the requirement for oral disclosures of proprietary information to be identified as such at the time of disclosure and documented in a written memorandum. The Law Explanation states that this change may hinder enforcement of trade secret protections under the DTSA, as it attempts to protect information that may not qualify as a trade secret, which typically requires efforts to maintain secrecy. Snippet 1 (18 USC 1839) provides definitions related to trade secrets. While it doesn't explicitly detail the requirements for maintaining secrecy, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation suggest that removing the identification and documentation requirements weakens trade secret protection and may contradict the intent, if not the explicit wording, of trade secret law. The change directly removes a safeguard that ensures the information is treated and considered proprietary, weakening its claim to trade secret status.

perturbation.original_text: 8. This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of Delaware, without regard to conflicts of law principles. Any dispute, controversy and/or difference which may arise between the Parties out of or in relation to or in connection with this Agreement, or the breach thereof, which cannot be settled by amicable mutual accord without undue delay, shall be filed in the courts of Delaware.
perturbation.changed_text: 8. This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of Delaware, without regard to conflicts of law principles. Any dispute, controversy and/or difference which may arise between the Parties out of or in relation to or in connection with this Agreement, or the breach thereof, which cannot be settled by amicable mutual accord without undue delay, shall be resolved through binding arbitration in a location of the Disclosing Party's choosing.
perturbation.explanation: The agreement specifies litigation in Delaware courts. Changing this to mandatory binding arbitration, especially where the location is chosen by one party, can conflict with state laws governing arbitration agreements and their enforceability. This can especially be the case if the arbitration clause is deemed unconscionable.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Delaware Uniform Arbitration Act provides a framework for arbitration agreements, including provisions for challenging and enforcing them. A clause that forces arbitration in a location solely determined by one party might be challenged under principles of unconscionability or fairness, conflicting with the Act's intent to provide a fair and balanced arbitration process.

Uniform Arbitration Act Â§ 5701. Effect of arbitration agreement. A written agreement to submit to arbitration any controversy existing at or arising after the effective date of the agreement is valid, enforceable and irrevocable, save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract, without regard to the justiciable character of the controversy, and confers jurisdiction on the Chancery Court of the State to enforce it and to enter judgment on an award. In determining any matter arising under this chapter, the Court shall not consider whether the claim with respect to which arbitration is sought is tenable, or otherwise pass upon the merits of the dispute. perturbation.justification: The original text mandates disputes be filed in Delaware courts, while the changed text mandates binding arbitration in a location chosen by the Disclosing Party. This contradicts the Delaware Uniform Arbitration Act, which intends to provide a fair and balanced arbitration process. The Law Explanation states the clause might be challenged under "unconscionability or fairness". Scraped Law Snippet 1, Â§ 5701 states that agreements are valid save upon such grounds as exist at law or in equity for the revocation of any contract. The unilateral selection of arbitration location introduces a potential for unfairness and thus a basis for revocation, creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 6. This Non-Disclosure Agreement shall remain in full force and effect from the effective date indicated above. Either party may terminate this Agreement by giving the other Party sixty (60) days’ written notice. However the confidentiality obligations and all powers, rights and duties provided herein shall extend for a period of five (5) years following the termination of this Agreement. Neither Party shall assign this Ag reement to any third party, without the prior written consent of the other Party.
perturbation.changed_text: 6. This Non-Disclosure Agreement shall remain in full force and effect from the effective date indicated above. Either party may terminate this Agreement by giving the other Party sixty (60) days’ written notice. However the confidentiality obligations and all powers, rights and duties provided herein shall extend indefinitely following the termination of this Agreement. Neither Party shall assign this Agreement to any third party, without the prior written consent of the other Party.
perturbation.explanation: The original agreement limits the confidentiality obligations to five years post-termination. Changing this to 'indefinitely' can be problematic under Delaware law, as perpetual restrictions are disfavored and may be deemed an unreasonable restraint of trade.
perturbation.law_citation: Delaware common law on Restraint of Trade
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware courts generally disfavor restraints of trade that are unlimited in time. An indefinite confidentiality obligation may be deemed an unreasonable restraint, especially if it prevents the Receiving Party from engaging in legitimate business activities after a reasonable period. The case law sets a precedent that overly broad restrictions are unenforceable.

perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the confidentiality obligations from a defined period of five years to 'indefinitely'. The Perturbation Explanation states that such indefinite restrictions are disfavored under Delaware law as they may be deemed an unreasonable restraint of trade. The Law Explanation reinforces this by stating that Delaware courts generally disfavor restraints of trade that are unlimited in time, particularly if they prevent legitimate business activities. Although the scraped snippets are unhelpful in this case, the change from a defined term to an indefinite term directly contradicts the legal principle against unreasonable restraints of trade, as explained, thus creating a significant legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 4. Immediately upon a request by the Disclosing Pa rty at any time, the Receiving Party will turn over to the Disclosing Party all Proprietary Inform ation of the Disclosing Party and all documents or media containing any such Proprietary Inform ation and any and all copies or extracts thereof or upon request of the Disclosing Party, the Receiving Party shall certify in writing that all materials containing Proprietary Information (incl uding all copies thereof) have been destroyed.
perturbation.changed_text: 4. Immediately upon a request by the Disclosing Party at any time, the Receiving Party will turn over to the Disclosing Party all Proprietary Information of the Disclosing Party and all documents or media containing any such Proprietary Information and any and all copies or extracts thereof. Destruction of Proprietary Information is strictly prohibited under any circumstance.
perturbation.explanation: The original clause allows for destruction of Proprietary Information upon request, offering flexibility. By explicitly prohibiting destruction under any circumstance, the modified clause introduces a contradiction. This could conflict with the principle of 'litigation hold' and discovery rules, where destruction of evidence can have legal ramifications, also conflicting with data retention regulations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure Rule 37(e)
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP Rule 37(e) addresses the failure to preserve electronically stored information in anticipation or conduct of litigation. While it doesn't mandate destruction, it provides a framework for sanctions if information is lost because a party failed to take reasonable steps to preserve it, especially when there's a duty to preserve. A blanket prohibition against destruction, even when legally permissible or required by data retention schedules, could be problematic and contradict accepted discovery practices. The new language could lead to issues during e-discovery if a party is forced to retain data they are otherwise legally permitted to destroy, or are required to destroy under other regulations (e.g. GDPR).
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 37. Failure to Make Disclosures or to Cooperate in Discovery; Sanctions | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 37. Failure to Make Disclosures or to Cooperate in Discovery; Sanctions (a) Motion for an Order Compelling Disclosure or Discovery. (1) In General. On notice to other parties and all affected persons, a party may move for an order compelling disclosure or discovery. The motion must include a certification that the movant has in good faith conferred or attempted to confer with the person or party failing to make disclosure or discovery in an effort to obtain it without court action. (2) Appropriate Court. A motion for an order to a party must be made in the court where the action is pending. A motion for an order to a nonparty must be made in the court where the discovery is or will be taken. (3) Specific Motions. (A) To Compel Disclosure. If a party fails to make a disclosure required by Rule 26(a) , any other party may move to compel disclosure and for appropriate sanctions. (B) To Compel a Discovery Response. A party seeking discovery may move for an order compelling an answer, designation, production, or inspection. This motion may be made if: (i) a deponent fails to answer a question asked under Rule 30 or 31 ; (ii) a corporation or other entity fails to make a designation under Rule 30(b)(6) or 31(a)(4) ; (iii) a party fails to answer an interrogatory submitted under Rule 33 ; or (iv) a party fails to produce documents or fails to respond that inspection will be permitted—or fails to permit inspection—as requested under Rule 34 . (C) Related to a Deposition. When taking an oral deposition, the party asking a question may complete or adjourn the examination before moving for an order. (4) Evasive or Incomplete Disclosure, Answer, or Response. For purposes of this subdivision (a), an evasive or incomplete disclosure, answer, or response must be treated as a failur

Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a direct contradiction by prohibiting the destruction of proprietary information under any circumstance. The original text allowed for destruction upon request. The Law Explanation highlights that FRCP Rule 37(e) provides a framework for sanctions if information is lost due to a failure to preserve it, especially when there's a duty to preserve. The changed text's blanket prohibition could conflict with accepted discovery practices and data retention schedules, creating a potential conflict with FRCP and other regulations, as it may force a party to retain or destroy data contrary to legal permissions or requirements. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions Rule 37 regarding failure to make disclosures or cooperate in discovery, further highlighting the importance of adhering to discovery rules, which the changed text could potentially violate.
